lessonTitle: Living with Less: The Minimalist Movement
lessonGoal: Students will read an informational text about minimalism, identify the main idea and supporting evidence, use cause-and-effect language, and write a short opinion paragraph.

warmUp.title: Warm-up
warmUp.instructions: Think about your own belongings. Answer the questions below. There are no right or wrong answers.
questions: What is one item you use every day? Why is it important to you?
Have you ever given away something you no longer needed? How did it feel?
In one sentence, what does "living with less" mean to you?

article.title: Why More People Are Choosing to Live with Less
article.source: Adapted for classroom use — OFS Grade 9 International English
paragraphs: In cities around the world, a growing number of people are choosing to own fewer possessions. This lifestyle, often called minimalism, is not simply about having an empty room. Instead, it is about keeping only what is useful, meaningful, or joyful.

Many minimalists say they started this change because they felt overwhelmed. Their homes were full of clothes they never wore and gadgets they rarely used. Shopping had become a habit, not a need. As a result, they decided to reduce clutter and spend money more carefully.

Living with less can also benefit the environment. When people buy fewer products, factories produce less waste and use fewer resources. Some families now repair old items instead of replacing them. Others borrow tools from neighbours rather than buying new ones.

However, minimalism does not look the same for everyone. A student might keep a small wardrobe and digital notes instead of paper. A family might focus on shared experiences, such as hiking or cooking together, rather than buying more toys. The goal is intentionality — making choices on purpose.

Critics argue that minimalism is a privilege. People with very little money do not always choose to own less; they simply cannot afford more. Because of this, teachers and writers remind us to respect different circumstances and avoid judging others.

vocabularyInContext.title: Vocabulary in Context
vocabularyInContext.instructions: Use the article to understand each word. Match the word to its meaning.

items.definition: a lifestyle focused on owning fewer possessions on purpose
items.sentence: Minimalism is not simply about having an empty room.

items.definition: a messy collection of things that are not organised
items.sentence: They decided to reduce clutter in their homes.

items.definition: doing something with a clear purpose or plan
items.sentence: The goal is intentionality — making choices on purpose.

items.definition: a special advantage that not everyone has
items.sentence: Critics argue that minimalism is a privilege.

mainIdeaQuestion.title: Main Idea
mainIdeaQuestion.question: What is the main idea of the article?
options: Minimalism means throwing away everything you own.
More people are choosing to live with less for personal and environmental reasons, but the lifestyle looks different for everyone.
Shopping is illegal in many cities around the world.
Families should never buy toys for their children.
mainIdeaQuestion.explanation: The article explains that more people are choosing minimalism for personal and environmental reasons, and that the lifestyle varies for different people.

questions.question: According to the article, why did many minimalists change their lifestyle?
options: They wanted to impress their neighbours.
They felt overwhelmed by too many possessions.
Governments forced them to sell their belongings.
questions.explanation: The article says many minimalists started this change because they felt overwhelmed by too many possessions.

questions.question: How can living with less help the environment?
options: It encourages people to buy more gadgets.
It can reduce waste and resource use.
It stops families from cooking at home.
questions.explanation: The article states that when people buy fewer products, factories produce less waste and use fewer resources.

questions.question: What concern do critics raise about minimalism?
options: It is too expensive for rich people.
It may ignore people who cannot afford to buy more.
It requires everyone to live in one room.
questions.explanation: Critics argue that minimalism is a privilege because some people cannot afford to buy more, not because they choose to own less.

evidenceFromText.title: Evidence from the Text
evidenceFromText.instructions: Find a sentence or phrase from the article that supports each statement.

prompts.statement: Minimalism is not the same for every person.
Some people change their habits because they feel stressed by clutter.

languageFocus.title: Language Focus
languageFocus.explanation: Writers use cause-and-effect language to explain why something happens or what happens as a result. Common connectors include because, as a result, therefore, and because of this.
examples: Many minimalists started this change because they felt overwhelmed.
As a result, they decided to reduce clutter.
Because of this, teachers remind us to respect different circumstances.
practice: Complete the sentence: People buy fewer products, ________ factories produce less waste.
Write one sentence using "because" about a habit you would like to change.

speakingPractice.title: Speaking Practice
speakingPractice.instructions: Discuss with a partner or record a short response (60–90 seconds).
prompts: Would you like to try minimalism? Why or why not?
What is one possession you could live without? Explain your choice.
How can schools encourage sustainable habits without judging students?

writingTask.title: Writing Task
writingTask.prompt: Write a short opinion paragraph (80–120 words) answering this question: Do you think minimalism is a good idea for teenagers? Give at least two reasons and use one cause-and-effect connector.
checklist: Clear opinion in the opening sentence
One cause-and-effect connector (because, as a result, therefore)

selfCheck.title: Self-check
selfCheck.instructions: Before you submit, review your work.
items: I identified the main idea of the article.
I answered the comprehension questions using the text.
I found evidence to support key statements.
I used cause-and-effect language in my writing.

answerKey.mainIdea: More people are choosing to live with less for personal and environmental reasons, but the lifestyle looks different for everyone.

readingComprehension.rc1: They felt overwhelmed by too many possessions.
readingComprehension.rc2: It can reduce waste and resource use.
readingComprehension.rc3: It may ignore people who cannot afford to buy more.

evidenceFromText.ev1: However, minimalism does not look the same for everyone.
evidenceFromText.ev2: Many minimalists say they started this change because they felt overwhelmed.

teacher.modelAnswer: I believe minimalism can be a good idea for teenagers because it teaches us to value experiences over possessions. Many students feel stressed when their rooms are cluttered, so owning fewer items can help them focus on school and hobbies. As a result, they may also waste less money on things they do not need. However, minimalism should be a choice, not a rule, because not everyone has the same resources. Overall, thoughtful minimalism can help teenagers live more intentionally.

rubric.excellent: Clear opinion with two strong, relevant reasons
rubric.developing: Opinion present but reasons are vague or off-topic

rubric.excellent: Uses cause-and-effect connector accurately; few errors
rubric.developing: Some connector use; errors do not block meaning

rubric.excellent: Logical paragraph with opening, support, and conclusion
rubric.developing: Some organisation; weak opening or ending

teacherNotes: Pre-teach vocabulary before the first reading if students are G1 level.
Pair students for evidence-finding: one reads aloud while the other highlights.
Remind students that evidence answers must quote or closely paraphrase the article.
For speaking, allow students to use L1 briefly to plan before responding in English.
Differentiate writing: G1 may write 60–80 words; G2 should aim for 100+ words.

teachingScript.script: Today we are reading about minimalism — choosing to live with less on purpose. Look at the warm-up questions. In pairs, share one item you use every day and why it matters.
Read the article silently first. Then we will read two paragraphs together. As you read, underline one sentence that surprises you.
Answer the main idea and comprehension questions. Use the text — do not guess. Be ready to point to the paragraph that helped you.
Notice the cause-and-effect connectors in the language focus box. In your writing, include at least one connector and two reasons for your opinion.
Use the self-check list. Submit your work. If time remains, volunteers can share one sentence from their opinion paragraph.
